title: Using AI to Let History Speak About Bank Runs - Liberty Street Economics
gist: Fed researchers used large language models to mine over 374 million digitized historical newspaper pages and built the most comprehensive database of U.S. bank runs (1863-1934), finding that most runs do not end in failure and that distress geography shifted westward with the banking frontier.

insights.claim: Researchers built a pipeline that starts from over 374 million digitized newspaper articles, narrows to about a million candidates via keyword search for terms like 'bank run,' 'deposit withdrawals,' 'suspended,' and 'closed its doors,' then feeds each candidate to multiple LLMs to extract structured facts: which bank, what happened, when, and how the bank and its stakeholders responded.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: A reusable template for turning unstructured historical/archival text at massive scale into structured panel data using LLMs — applicable to other historical financial-event mining (defaults, panics, currency crises) beyond bank runs.
insights.quote: we use large language models (LLMs) to extract information on bank runs from millions of digitized historical newspaper pages, creating the most comprehensive database of bank runs in U.S. history.
insights.locator: How Newspapers Can Help Us to Understand Bank Runs

insights.claim: LLMs succeed where rigid keyword search fails because nineteenth-century prose describes the same event many different ways (e.g., depositors 'clamoring for their money' with no use of the word 'run'), while also filtering out false positives like a 'ship runs into a riverbank' or a bank owner who 'runs for office.'
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a concrete case where LLM contextual understanding materially outperforms keyword/regex-based text mining for noisy historical or informal language — relevant to any NLP pipeline built on archival or unstructured text.
insights.quote: False positives can occur when articles describe how “a ship runs into a riverbank” or the owner of a bank “runs for office.”

insights.claim: To ensure data quality the authors deliberately ran multiple LLMs against each article, flagged disagreements between models for extra scrutiny, and validated the automated output against hand-coded samples, finding high agreement.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: A concrete quality-assurance recipe (multi-model ensemble + human-labeled validation set) for anyone using LLMs to generate research-grade structured datasets from text.
insights.quote: We further validate our LLM classifications against hand-coded samples and find high agreement, giving us confidence that the automated approach produces reliable data at scale.

insights.claim: The complete bank-run database is published publicly at finhist.com/bank-runs, browsable by state, city, year, or bank type, with each episode linked back to the original source newspaper articles.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: An open, granular historical financial-distress dataset now exists for outside researchers, quants, or policy analysts to build long-run panic/contagion models or stress-test modern banking-crisis frameworks against pre-FDIC history.
insights.locator: The complete database is publicly available

themes.theme: Bank Runs vs. Bank Failures Taxonomy

insights.claim: The authors define three distinct distress event types — bank runs, suspensions, and failures — and note that by construction any bank failure is also a suspension, since a bank must suspend operations before or at the moment of failure.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: Gives investors/analysts a cleaner conceptual vocabulary for distinguishing liquidity events (runs, suspensions) from solvency events (failures) when reading historical or modern banking-crisis narratives.

insights.claim: Contrary to the common conflation of 'bank run' with 'bank failure,' the data show 1,906 run episodes that did not end in failure versus 1,515 that did — meaning the majority of documented bank runs between 1863 and 1934 were survived, not fatal.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: Suggests historical base rates overstate the lethality of runs; useful prior for modeling how modern deposit runs (digital-era, faster-moving) might still be survivable with adequate backstops rather than automatically fatal.
insights.quote: A first major insight from our data is that the majority of bank runs do not involve failure. We find 1,906 runs without failure and 1,515 runs with failure.

insights.claim: Across the full 1863-1934 sample the dataset records 13,069 suspension episodes and 10,330 failure episodes, versus roughly 3,400 identified run episodes — indicating that most newspaper-documented bank distress overall involved a failure, even though most runs specifically did not.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: Highlights that runs are a relatively small subset of total historical bank distress; failure risk analysis needs to look beyond run episodes alone to capture the broader universe of bank closures.
insights.quote: We also find 13,069 episodes involving a suspension, and 10,330 episodes involving a failure. Thus, the majority of distress episodes recorded in newspapers involve a bank failure.

insights.claim: The rate of bank runs relative to the total number of national banks spikes distinctly in 1873, 1884, 1893, 1907 (to a lesser extent), and during the Great Depression, matching the crisis years already established in the historical banking-panic literature.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: Validates the new LLM-extracted dataset against known crisis chronology, giving confidence the data can be used for further quantitative work on panic dynamics.
insights.quote: The rate of runs spikes during the major crises years: 1873, 1884, 1893, 1907 (to a lesser extent), and the Great Depression.

insights.claim: Runs without failure are especially concentrated in major panic years — for example the Panic of 1893 produced an unusually large number of runs that were resolved via temporary suspension rather than permanent failure.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: Implies that during systemic (rather than idiosyncratic) panics, temporary suspension was a more common resolution mechanism than outright failure — a pattern relevant to designing modern circuit-breaker/suspension tools during systemic stress.
insights.locator: The chart also distinguishes between bank runs with and without failure

insights.claim: The Panic of 1893 was structurally different from the other National Banking Era panics: it originated in the interior of the country rather than New York, and was tied to distress in railroads, silver mines, and agriculture rather than a money-center shock.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: Shows financial contagion can originate from real-economy/commodity shocks in the periphery rather than always from a money-center liquidity event — a reminder not to assume every systemic crisis will start in the financial capital.
insights.quote: The 1893 crisis, in contrast to the other panics of the National Banking Era, originated in the interior and saw more runs in the West and South, consistent with distress in railroads, silver mines, and agriculture in that panic.

insights.claim: The geographic center of bank distress shifted westward over the decades: early episodes clustered on the Eastern Seaboard (New York, Philadelphia), but by the 1890s runs were appearing in the Great Plains, Mountain West, and Pacific Coast as the banking system expanded into new agricultural and mining frontiers.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: Financial-system geography tracks the frontier of real economic expansion; a template for thinking about how modern financial contagion follows the geography of newer/less-diversified sectors (e.g., regional banks exposed to a single industry or region).

insights.claim: Nationwide systemic panics spread as visible waves radiating outward from money-center cities — the Panic of 1873 clustered first in New York before radiating to Chicago, St. Louis, and New Orleans, and the Panic of 1907 again started in New York but spread quickly through the national correspondent banking network.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: Confirms correspondent-banking interconnectedness as a contagion transmission channel — directly analogous to modern interbank/counterparty-network contagion risk analysis.
insights.locator: During the Panic of 1873, for instance

insights.claim: The 1920s, popularly remembered as a decade of prosperity, actually show a steady stream of bank failures concentrated in the agricultural Midwest and South on the geographic map, foreshadowing the much larger surge in bank failures during the Great Depression.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: A cautionary data point that aggregate/national prosperity narratives can mask persistent regional financial deterioration that later becomes systemic — relevant to reading current 'resilient economy' headlines against pockets of regional stress (e.g., agricultural or commodity-exposed regional banks).
insights.quote: The 1920s, often remembered as a decade of prosperity, are marked on the map by a steady stream of bank failures in the agricultural Midwest and South, foreshadowing the surge in bank failures during the Great Depression.
insights.locator: Third, the map reveals the regional character

insights.claim: The authors state that a companion paper will use this newly built dataset to investigate when and why historical bank runs have led to bank failure, and to draw out implications for current policy discussions.
insights.speaker: trevordelaney
insights.so_what: Signals a forthcoming follow-on study that could directly inform bank-regulation and deposit-insurance policy debates by identifying which conditions historically converted a run into a failure.
insights.quote: In our companion post, we show how we use the data to investigate when and why runs have historically led to bank failure, and discuss how these insights affect policy discussions.

top_entities: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
